expectations:
- MUST be in Spanish

      
  - It should assume learners start with no prior knowledge of the subject
  - Should create a progressive learning path going from basic/beginner to mastery of the subject
  - By the end of this course, they should be able to lead very complex projects and tasks in this field
  - They should be prepared for certifications or advanced studies in this subject like a master's degree or PhD
  - Should include **EVERYTHING** a student needs to be at the top 1% of the field
  - Should follow the language specified by language parameter
  - Should follow title and description guidelines: no fluff, be concise, straight to the point
  - Should cover latest trends in the field
  - Prefer concept names over vendor names in chapter titles/descriptions (e.g., "Package management" instead of "npm"). Ecosystem-level tools (npm, yarn, Redux, Zustand) should be avoided in titles
    - Exception: foundational tools that ARE the course subject matter are allowed (e.g., "Git" in a programming course, "Node.js" in a web dev course, "Docker" in a DevOps course). The test: would a chapter about this subject be incomplete without mentioning this tool?
    - Vendor name usage is a minor issue — it affects title quality but not pedagogical content. Do NOT treat it as a major error
  - This is an eval system for a learning platform, of course the definition of "ready for a job" doesn't mean they have the legal requirements to work in the field (eg. medical license, law license, etc.). That's not important here, we're assessing if the course prepares the student with the necessary knowledge and skills.
  - You don't need to evaluate the output format here, just focus on the chapter content quality.
  - Titles should be concise and straight to the point, no fluff/filler words. For example:
    - Just "HTML" is better than "HTML Structure and Semantics" (structure and semantics are implied if this is the only HTML chapter). Similarly, if we only have one CSS chapter, just "CSS" is better than "CSS Styling and Layout".
    - "Front-end frameworks" is better than "Front-End UI Frameworks Overview" (too verbose) or "Front-End Frameworks: Core Concepts" (core concepts is unnecessary fluff)
    - "Relational Databases" is better than "Relational Databases and SQL" (SQL is implied, no need to mention)
    - "Introduction to Java" is better than "Introduction to Java and Setting Up Your Development Environment" (too verbose)
    - "Lean Startup" is better than "The Lean Startup Methodology: An Overview" (too verbose)
    - "Introduction to X" is valid for titles of introductory chapters but we should avoid those words in descriptions
  - Similarly, descriptions should be concise and straight to the point, no fluff/filler words. For example:
    - "Styling with CSS: Selectors, properties, the box model, Flexbox, CSS Grid, and cascade principles." is better than "Master styling and layout with CSS, including selectors, properties, the box model, Flexbox, CSS Grid, and cascade principles." - "Master" is fluff, and so are words like "learn", "understand", "explore", etc.
    - "Properties of matter, states, and phase transitions." is better than "Explore the definition of Chemistry, properties of matter, states, and phase transitions." - "Explore the definition of Chemistry" are filler/unnecessary words.
  - Don't add assessment-style chapters: no final projects, capstone assignments, exercises, quizzes, or "test your knowledge" chapters. A theoretical synthesis chapter (e.g., "Design Patterns in Architecture") is fine even if it synthesizes prior material — the rule targets assessment/project chapters, not synthesis of knowledge. If a chapter uses the word "capstone" but is theoretical (not an assignment), treat it as a minor issue at most, not a major error
  - It shouldn't mention in title or description references to 1% or similar instructions about being top 1%

rankings.reasoning: Model C provides the most comprehensive and well-structured curriculum with 53 chapters. It excels in several areas: (1) Excellent progressive structure from fundamentals through advanced topics; (2) Outstanding granularity in analytical chemistry (separate chapters for IR/Raman, UV-Vis, NMR, mass spectrometry, crystallography, separations, electroanalysis, metrology); (3) Covers advanced/modern topics like supramolecular chemistry, photochemistry, automation/AI in chemistry, catalysis and surfaces; (4) Includes practical professional skills (safety, ethics, scientific communication, career); (5) Descriptions are concise and follow the no-fluff guidelines well; (6) Titles are generally concise and professional; (7) Covers equilibrium of phases and process separation, which many others miss; (8) Includes ética/cumplimiento as a separate chapter. Minor issues: some titles could be slightly more concise (e.g., 'Fundamentos de la química y medición'), and the 'Automatización e IA en química' chapter is forward-looking which is good. Overall this is the most complete curriculum that would genuinely prepare someone for top-level work.

Model H provides an extremely comprehensive curriculum with 93 chapters covering enormous breadth and depth. Strengths: (1) Exceptional granularity - separate chapters for nearly every major subtopic; (2) Includes unique and important chapters like 'Matemáticas para química', 'Física para química', quimiometría, muestreo/preparación de muestras, análisis térmico, productos naturales, biofísica estructural, química atmosférica separately from environmental chemistry; (3) Very strong analytical chemistry coverage; (4) Excellent organic chemistry breakdown; (5) Includes machine learning, data management, reproducibility; (6) Strong safety/regulatory coverage with separate process safety and lab safety chapters. However, some weaknesses: (1) At 93 chapters, the granularity is perhaps excessive - some chapters could be combined (e.g., separate chapters for 'Química aromática' and 'Heterociclos' in organic, separate oxidation/reduction chapter); (2) The math and physics prerequisite chapters, while useful, may be overly broad for a chemistry course; (3) 'Química de alimentos' and 'Química forense' are not in Model H (chemistry of food is there but forensic is not, unlike Model G). Descriptions are generally concise and well-written. The sheer depth is impressive but borders on being a multi-year PhD program rather than a single course.

Model G provides an extremely comprehensive curriculum with 65 chapters. Strengths: (1) Excellent depth across all major chemistry subdisciplines; (2) Very thorough organic chemistry coverage including pericyclic reactions, carbenes/nitrenes; (3) Includes unique topics like food chemistry, forensic chemistry, radiochemistry, bioinorganic chemistry, organometallic chemistry; (4) Good coverage of computational chemistry; (5) Includes energy chemistry as a separate chapter; (6) Lanthanides/actinides get their own chapter. Weaknesses: (1) Some descriptions are quite long and detailed, almost too verbose for descriptions (e.g., the cinética chapter description); (2) Some titles could be more concise (e.g., 'Enlace químico: iónico, covalente y metálico' - just 'Enlace químico' would suffice); (3) Mentions specific programming languages (Python, R) in a chapter title/description, which goes against vendor neutrality; (4) Some chapters have very long descriptions that border on being too detailed. Overall very strong coverage but slightly less polished in formatting.

Model A provides a solid curriculum with 45 chapters. Strengths: (1) Good logical progression from fundamentals to advanced topics; (2) Covers key areas including coordination chemistry, synthesis, spectroscopy, computational chemistry, medicinal chemistry, nanochemistry, catalysis; (3) Includes career chapter; (4) Descriptions are concise and well-written; (5) Titles are generally clean and professional. Weaknesses: (1) Less granular than Models C, H, or G in analytical chemistry - only one chapter for 'Química analítica'; (2) Missing some advanced topics like supramolecular chemistry, photochemistry, symmetry/group theory, food chemistry, forensic chemistry; (3) Spectroscopy coverage is somewhat limited (one chapter vs. separate IR, NMR, MS chapters in better models); (4) Missing equilibrium of phases; (5) Nuclear chemistry coverage is adequate but not as deep. The curriculum is good but doesn't quite reach the depth needed for 'top 1%' mastery compared to the best models.

Model D provides a well-rounded curriculum with 41 chapters. Strengths: (1) Good coverage of major areas; (2) Includes important chapters like 'Calidad y regulación', 'Diseño experimental y estadística', 'Nanotecnología química', 'Catálisis'; (3) Clean, concise titles and descriptions; (4) Includes career chapter; (5) The 'Temas avanzados y emergentes' chapter captures modern trends. Weaknesses: (1) Organic chemistry is condensed into just two chapters ('Química orgánica I' and 'Química orgánica II'), which is far too compressed for such a vast topic; (2) Spectroscopy doesn't get individual technique chapters; (3) Missing symmetry/group theory; (4) Missing photochemistry; (5) 'Fisicoquímica' as a single chapter is too broad - should be broken into quantum, statistical mechanics, etc.; (6) Missing supramolecular chemistry; (7) The breadth is adequate but depth is lacking in several key areas. The organic chemistry compression is a significant weakness.

Model E provides a solid curriculum with 45 chapters. Strengths: (1) Good progressive structure; (2) Includes quantum chemistry and physical chemistry molecular chapters separately; (3) Good analytical chemistry coverage with separate classical and instrumental chapters plus chromatography; (4) Includes surfaces and colloids, nanoscience, computational chemistry; (5) Good organic chemistry coverage with stereochemistry, mechanisms, and advanced synthesis; (6) Career chapter included. Weaknesses: (1) Missing symmetry/group theory; (2) Missing supramolecular chemistry; (3) Missing catalysis as a dedicated chapter; (4) Missing photochemistry; (5) Descriptions occasionally use slightly verbose phrasing; (6) Some chapter titles mention specific techniques that could be more general (e.g., mentioning GC, HPLC in description of chromatography chapter is fine but the title 'Métodos cromatográficos y de separación' is adequate). Overall a competent curriculum but missing some advanced topics that would be expected for top-level mastery.

Model B provides a curriculum with 41 chapters. Strengths: (1) Clean, concise titles; (2) Good logical progression; (3) Includes separate chapters for solubility equilibria; (4) Reasonable coverage of major areas. Weaknesses: (1) The shortest and least comprehensive among all models; (2) Missing many important advanced topics: no symmetry/group theory, no photochemistry, no supramolecular chemistry, no catalysis dedicated chapter, no medicinal chemistry, no nuclear chemistry detail beyond basics; (3) Organic chemistry coverage is limited - missing detailed carbonyl chemistry, advanced synthesis strategies; (4) Missing computational chemistry depth; (5) Missing polymer chemistry as separate chapter (only mentioned in 'Química de Materiales'); (6) No statistical/data analysis chapter; (7) Missing ethics/professional responsibility chapter; (8) Spectroscopy is condensed into just 'Elucidación Estructural'; (9) The career chapter description is too brief. This curriculum would not adequately prepare someone for top-level mastery - significant gaps in advanced topics.

Model F provides an extensive curriculum with 67 chapters but has significant issues. Strengths: (1) Extremely comprehensive coverage including many unique topics (high-throughput screening, lab infrastructure design, instrument maintenance, entrepreneurship, mentorship); (2) Good granularity in spectroscopy and computational chemistry; (3) Includes AI/ML, data science, and modern trends. Weaknesses: (1) Many chapters venture far outside chemistry into general professional development territory ('Liderazgo, mentoría y gestión de talento', 'Búsqueda de financiación y colaboración científica', 'Emprendimiento, negocio y comercialización en química', 'Diseño, montaje y mantenimiento de infraestructuras de laboratorio', 'Operación y mantenimiento de instrumentación analítica') - these feel like padding rather than chemistry content; (2) Two separate career chapters ('Transición a la carrera profesional' and 'Búsqueda de empleo, entrevistas y avance profesional') is excessive; (3) The last chapter 'Plan de maestría profesional y trayectoria hacia la excelencia' sounds like a capstone/summary chapter and references 'excelencia' which borders on the prompt's instruction about not mentioning top 1% language; (4) Missing some core chemistry topics while including peripheral ones - e.g., stoichiometry is covered but stereochemistry doesn't get its own chapter; (5) Some titles are verbose ('Química y física de la luz (fotones, fotoquímica y fotofísica)'); (6) The balance between chemistry content and professional/business content is off for a chemistry course. Despite breadth, the prioritization is questionable.

Model H provides an exceptionally comprehensive and rigorous curriculum (86 chapters) that perfectly aligns with the prompt's demand to take a learner from 'no knowledge' to the 'top 1%' and 'PhD level'. Notably, it recognizes that mastering chemistry from absolute zero requires foundational mathematics and physics, and includes these at the beginning. It then progresses through a highly granular path covering undergraduate, graduate, and highly specialized topics (such as cheminformatics, ML in chemistry, data reproducibility, etc.). The titles and descriptions strictly follow the 'no fluff' rule, using concise noun-based lists.

Model F is outstanding, providing 62 chapters with an excellent mix of rigorous scientific theory, advanced applications, and critical professional skills (such as lab design, scientific funding, IP/patents, leadership, and entrepreneurship in chemistry). It strictly adheres to the formatting and 'no fluff' constraints. It is slightly less granular in the hard sciences than Model H but excels in the professional development aspect.

Model G offers a very strong 62-chapter curriculum that covers a wide array of specialized chemical fields, including forensic chemistry, food chemistry, and applied programming/statistics. It perfectly follows the formatting guidelines, includes a career chapter, and avoids fluff. It ranks slightly below F and H because its progression into advanced topics isn't quite as deep as H.

Model C provides a highly rigorous, 51-chapter curriculum. It covers advanced topics like supramolecular chemistry, AI, and electroanalysis, reaching a PhD level of depth. The descriptions and titles are exceptionally concise and well-written. It is slightly less expansive than H, F, and G, but still an excellent curriculum.

Model E offers a solid 46-chapter curriculum that covers standard undergraduate chemistry and dips into some graduate-level topics. It follows all rules, including the career chapter and avoiding fluff, but lacks the extreme comprehensiveness expected for a 'top 1%' curriculum compared to the higher-ranked models.

Model A provides a 45-chapter curriculum that is essentially a standard university chemistry degree. It follows all formatting rules and constraints, but it does not reach the 'PhD' or 'top 1%' depth requested by the prompt as thoroughly as the top models.

Model D provides a 41-chapter curriculum. While it is a good, well-structured course without fluff, it is significantly shorter and less detailed than the top models, missing the extreme depth required to prepare someone for a PhD or the top 1% of the field.

Model B provides a 41-chapter curriculum. Similar to Model D, it covers the basics and standard undergraduate topics well, but lacks the expansiveness, granularity, and advanced topics required to fully satisfy the prompt's ambitious goals.

La cobertura es la más completa, progresiva y cercana a una ruta de no conocimiento a dominio avanzado. Incluye fundamentos, orgánica, inorgánica, analítica, bioquímica, materiales, procesos, seguridad, ética, computacional, tendencias modernas e inserción profesional. También incorpora temas de frontera actuales como automatización, IA, química de flujo, laboratorios autónomos y ciencia de datos, lo que cumple muy bien con el requisito de estar al día. Los títulos y descripciones son mayormente concisos y profesionales. Pequeños puntos mejorables: algunos títulos/descripciones son algo densos o avanzados para una etapa temprana, y hay cierta sofisticación excesiva en algunos capítulos iniciales, pero en conjunto es el mejor alineado con el objetivo.

Excelente amplitud y profundidad, con una estructura muy sólida y rigurosa. Destaca por incluir prerrequisitos útiles para dominio real de la química como matemáticas y física, además de una cobertura muy fuerte en analítica, fisicoquímica, orgánica, inorgánica, bioquímica, materiales, procesos, seguridad, regulación, datos y ML. Está muy actualizado y prepara bien para estudios avanzados. La principal debilidad es que esos capítulos de matemáticas y física pueden alejarse un poco del formato esperado de capítulos de química, y algunos títulos/descripciones son más largos o técnicos de lo ideal. Aun así, la calidad curricular es sobresaliente.

Muy completo y bien secuenciado desde fundamentos hasta áreas especializadas. Cubre casi todo lo importante para una formación de élite: inorgánica, orgánica, analítica, fisicoquímica, bioquímica, ambiental, materiales, computacional, seguridad, ética y carrera. También incluye tendencias modernas como machine learning, bioinformática química y energía. Su principal limitación es cierta inclinación a un estilo enciclopédico de química universitaria tradicional más que una arquitectura curricular moderna; además, algunos títulos y descripciones son demasiado largos y cargados, con algo de redundancia entre capítulos. Aun así, es una propuesta excelente.

Cobertura extraordinariamente amplia, probablemente la más extensa de todas, con muchos temas avanzados y profesionales: liderazgo, gestión de proyectos, transferencia tecnológica, emprendimiento, IA, ciencia de datos y automatización. Eso ayuda mucho en el objetivo de liderazgo profesional. Sin embargo, se penaliza por no seguir bien algunas reglas del prompt: varios títulos son demasiado verbosos, uno incluye paréntesis explicativos innecesarios, el capítulo final 'Plan de maestría profesional y trayectoria hacia la excelencia' suena más a síntesis/meta-ruta que a contenido disciplinar, y hay algo de deriva hacia gestión/negocio no estrictamente química. También usa más anglicismos y vendor/regulatory naming de lo deseable. Aun así, la calidad global sigue siendo alta.

Muy buena cobertura, bien progresiva y en español claro. Incluye fundamentos, orgánica, analítica, fisicoquímica, bioquímica, materiales, computacional, procesos industriales, seguridad, calidad, comunicación e inserción profesional. Se mantiene bastante conciso y profesional. Sus debilidades frente a las mejores opciones son que omite o trata menos algunos temas de frontera y especialización profunda, como catálisis como capítulo independiente, automatización/IA, teoría de grupos, fotoquímica, superficies y tendencias emergentes. Aun así, cumple muy bien y sería un currículo sólido de alto nivel.

Buen currículo general con progresión lógica y cobertura razonablemente amplia. Incluye seguridad, laboratorio, regulación, computacional, materiales, ambiental, industrial y carrera. También añade temas emergentes al final. No obstante, la profundidad es menor que en los mejores modelos: varias áreas complejas quedan agrupadas de forma demasiado amplia, por ejemplo fisicoquímica en un solo capítulo y orgánica en solo dos capítulos, lo que reduce la sensación de dominio exhaustivo. Cumple bien, pero no alcanza del todo el nivel de máxima completitud exigido.

Muy amplio y fuerte en contenido disciplinar, con buena cobertura de áreas centrales y varias especializadas como nanoquímica, química medicinal y computacional. También incluye seguridad, herramientas digitales, comunicación científica y carrera. Sin embargo, tiene algunos problemas de adherencia: varios títulos y descripciones son más largos de lo necesario, nombra herramientas y bases de datos específicas (SciFinder, Reaxys, ChemDraw) pese a la preferencia por neutralidad, y el capítulo 'Ingeniería química y procesos industriales' mezcla un área completa en un solo capítulo. Además, faltan algunos temas modernos explícitos como automatización/IA y una capa más fuerte de estadística/datos avanzados en comparación con los mejores.

Es claro, ordenado y correcto en lo fundamental, con buena progresión desde conceptos básicos hasta algunos temas avanzados. Incluye carrera profesional y seguridad, lo cual ayuda a cumplir el prompt. Sin embargo, se queda corto en profundidad y exhaustividad para el estándar de 'todo lo necesario' y preparación para estudios avanzados de máximo nivel. Faltan varias áreas relevantes o están demasiado resumidas: síntesis orgánica avanzada, química computacional moderna, estadística/metrología más robusta, procesos y escalado con más detalle, comunicación científica, tendencias recientes, automatización, ciencia de datos y varias subáreas avanzadas. Es bueno, pero notablemente menos completo.
